one_line_takeaway: QueryIPI shows that conditioning a malicious tool-description payload on a coding agent's leaked system prompt (its 'system invariant') rather than on the user's query turns indirect prompt injection from an unreliable, query-triggered attack (best baseline ASR 0.50 simulated / 0.02 real-world) into a query-agnostic attack that succeeds regardless of user intent (QueryIPI ASR up to 0.87 simulated / 0.50 real-world), and this holds up even against tested prompt-injection defenses (Sandwich, Spotlighting) and perplexity-based detection.
key_finding: QueryIPI substantially outperforms all four query-specific/tool-description baselines and demonstrates real-world transfer. Main comparison (Table 1, simulated agents, average ASR over 5 agents): AgentDojo 0.02, InjecAgent 0.00, MCPTox 0.00, TIP 0.50 (best baseline), vs. QueryIPI 0.70 (n=2 training samples), 0.82 (n=4), 0.87 (n=8) -- QueryIPI already beats the best baseline with only 2 training samples and surpasses it on every individual agent at n=4/8. RQ2 (Table 2): removing the internal prompt entirely drops average ASR to 0.20 (from 0.71 with a leaked-but-non-identical 'partial prompt'), showing the system-invariant is the dominant driver of attack success, and that an exact up-to-date prompt is not required -- an outdated public leak suffices. RQ3 (Table 3, real-world agents, num=8 payload): QueryIPI achieves average ASR 0.50 (peaking at Trae 0.72 and Cursor 0.63) versus TIP's 0.02 average (never exceeding 0.03 on any agent) -- over an order of magnitude higher, though QueryIPI's ASR degrades from the simulated 0.87 to 0.50 in deployment (attributed to proprietary defenses and sim-to-real gap). RQ4: prevention defenses provide only marginal mitigation -- Sandwich Defense ASR 0.82, Spotlighting-Datamarking 0.52, Spotlighting-Delimiting 0.74, Spotlighting-Encoding 0.84; detection via perplexity shows QueryIPI payloads remain fluent (PPL 59.04, Window PPL 130.98) versus a cited baseline of real-world MCP tool perplexity at 1423.27/1423.31, making statistical anomaly detection ineffective. RQ5 (Table 4, cross-backend transfer): QueryIPI achieves 0.26 average ASR across GPT-5/Grok-4/Gemini-2.5-pro backends versus TIP's 0.10 (more than double), though transfer is uneven -- Cline+GPT-5 reaches 0.95 for QueryIPI, while Copilot transfer collapses to near 0% across new backends, attributed to Copilot's unstructured single-line tool-definition format requiring model-specific tuning.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'query-agnostic Indirect Prompt Injection' (IPI) as a new attack paradigm against LLM-based coding agents (e.g., Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, Copilot, Trae), and QueryIPI, an automated black-box optimization framework that crafts a malicious external tool description which reliably triggers a targeted malicious action (arbitrary command execution) under ANY user query, not just a specific triggering query as required by prior IPI attacks. The key idea is to condition the attack on the agent's 'system invariant' -- its system prompt and internal tool descriptions, which are always present in context regardless of the user's query -- rather than on the variable user query itself. QueryIPI uses an iterative evolutionary/prompt-based search: a 'Mutation LLM' generates and reflectively refines candidate malicious tool descriptions using two phases (initial seed generation aligned to the agent's native tool-description style, then reflective optimization using past iteration scores and sampled agent responses, but withholding the user queries that produced them to avoid overfitting), scored by a 'Judge LLM' that assigns a continuous 0-100 score per response, summed across the training query set.
threat_model: Attacker has no access to the victim coding agent's runtime state but knows its specific version, so can set up a local copy of the same version to test/optimize the exploit offline. The attacker crafts a malicious external tool (tool_name, description, args) and tricks the victim agent into adding it to its toolset via package/name spoofing or other supply-chain vectors (paper cites prior work on such vectors but does not itself execute a supply-chain delivery -- it assumes the tool is already integrated: T' = T ∪ {t_mal}). The attack goal is formalized as: the compromised agent A^{T'}_{psys}(q) ≡ b_mal for all q in the space of possible user queries Q -- i.e., the malicious behavior fires unconditionally rather than only on a specific trigger query, unlike prior query-specific IPI (AgentDojo, InjecAgent, MCPTox, and tool-description-channel attacks like Tool Poisoning). The paper assumes the attacker can obtain the target agent's internal system prompt/tool descriptions ('system invariant'), justified via: open-source agents (e.g., Cline), prompt-extraction attacks (cites RepeatLeakage <10% failure rate, and a multi-turn method reaching 99.9% success on GPT-4), and public leak repositories (x1xhlol/system-prompts-and-models-of-ai-tools, which the paper itself draws from for its 5 simulated agents). The specific attack payload targets the agent's native command-execution tool (e.g., execute_command).
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) the threat model assumes the attacker can introduce a custom tool into the victim's toolset (via supply-chain/spoofing vectors that are cited but not themselves evaluated or executed in this paper); (2) the attack architecturally requires the target system to support user-defined tools whose descriptions are directly incorporated into the agent's context -- it is not universal to all agent architectures; (3) the method needs a capable backend LLM to act as the mutator, and while safety guardrails on that mutator LLM are a potential obstacle, the authors note jailbreaking techniques could bypass them (not tested in this paper). Additional limitations evident from the results themselves: real-world ASR (0.50 average) is markedly lower than simulated ASR (0.87 average) for the same payload, indicating a real sim-to-real gap from proprietary/undisclosed defenses; cross-backend transfer is highly agent-dependent and can collapse to near-zero (Copilot); and the RQ2 'partial prompt' condition, while promising for attacker practicality, was tested only in the simulated environment, not against real-world agents.

datasets_used: Command Injection Payload List (open-source; 10 distinct commands selected as malicious payload targets, listed in paper's Table/Appendix F: e.g., 'ls -l /home/*', 'whoami', 'sleep 5', 'netstat -an', a perl memory-stress one-liner, 'ping -n 30 127.0.0.1', 'uname -a', 'which wget', '/usr/bin/id', '/bin/ls -al')

LMSYS-Chat-1M (Zheng et al. 2024) -- large-scale real-world LLM conversation dataset; queries randomly sampled for QueryIPI training (2/4/8 samples) and testing (10 queries per command x 10 commands = 100 test cases)
quantitative_results: Table 1 average ASR across 5 simulated agents: AgentDojo 0.02, InjecAgent 0.00, MCPTox 0.00, TIP 0.50, QueryIPI n=2 0.70, n=4 0.82, n=8 0.87
Table 2 (RQ2) average ASR: w/o internal prompt 0.20, with partial (leaked, non-identical) internal prompt 0.71
Table 3 (RQ3) real-world agent average ASR: QueryIPI (num=8) 0.50 (Cursor 0.63, Copilot 0.41, Cline 0.34, Trae 0.72, Windsurf 0.42) vs. TIP 0.02 (max 0.03 on any agent)
RQ4 defense-robustness average ASR: Sandwich Defense 0.82, Spotlighting-Datamarking 0.52, Spotlighting-Delimiting 0.74, Spotlighting-Encoding 0.84
RQ4 detection: QueryIPI PPL 59.04 and Window PPL 130.98, vs. cited real-world MCP tool baseline PPL 1423.27 / Window PPL 1423.31

Table 4 (RQ5) cross-backend-LLM average ASR: QueryIPI 0.26 (GPT-5 0.40, Grok-4 0.21, Gemini-2.5-pro 0.16) vs. TIP 0.10 (GPT-5 0.13, Grok-4 0.08, Gemini-2.5-pro 0.09); per-agent extremes: Cline+GPT-5 QueryIPI 0.95, Copilot near 0% across all non-original backends
Judge LLM calibration against human annotators (152 data points): average Spearman's rho = 0.7790, average Kendall's tau = 0.6975
Optimization hyperparameters: 20 generations, 2 new variants per generation; mutator LLM temperature 1, target agent and judge LLM temperature 0
baselines_compared: AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al. 2024) -- tool-return-channel IPI, relocated into tool description for comparability
InjecAgent (Zhan et al. 2024) -- tool-return-channel IPI, relocated into tool description
MCPTox (Wang et al. 2025a) -- tool-description-channel tool-poisoning attack
TIP / TIPExploit (Xie et al. 2025) -- tool-description-channel attack, evaluated in its RCE-1 (tool-description-only) setting; the best-performing baseline overall
QueryIPI without internal prompt (ablation, used as a stand-in for automated iterative methods since ToolHijacker was excluded from the main comparison)

ToolHijacker (Shi et al. 2025) -- explicitly excluded from the main comparison because it requires a task-specific 'shadow task' incompatible with the query-agnostic setting and needs manual intervention without internal-prompt knowledge
